source_metadata.title_raw: Lacey Keys, Esq. - A-19-051 - June 21, 2019 - Sacramento

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 21, 2019

Lacey Keys, Esq.
Olson Hagel & Fishburn, LLP
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 400
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-051

Dear Ms. Keys:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign and lobbying
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Because your inquiry is general in nature, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice. Furthermore, we do not issue advice relating to past conduct. (Regulation 18239.)

QUESTIONS

1. As fiscal sponsors of various individual "projects," is the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy
required to report as a lobbyist employer if a project uses funds for lobbying activity?

2. If a project makes an in-kind contribution must the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy report
the contribution or must the individual project disclose the contribution?

3. Does a project become a recipient committee if it continues to make in-kind contributions?

4. If Tides Advocacy only spends nondonor funds on contributions going forward, should
Tides Advocacy remain registered as a general-purpose recipient or may it report as a major donor?

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18998 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. A-19-051
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. Your facts state that the Tides Center and Tides Advocacy solely provide back-office administrative and compliance services for multiple individually controlled projects. Under Section 82039.5 and the facts provided, it is permissible that an individual project report as a lobbyist employer, in lieu of the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy if the project : (a) employs one or more lobbyists for economic consideration, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action, or (b) contracts for the services of a lobbying firm for economic consideration, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expense[s], for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action.

2. As a multipurpose organization under Section 84222, the fiscal sponsor, either the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy, must report all campaign activity by any of its individual projects including all in-kind contributions.

3. The individual project does not have to register as a recipient committee as the fiscal sponsor, either the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy, must report the project's campaign activity as a multipurpose organization pursuant to Section 84222.

4. If Tides Advocacy uses only available nondonor funds to make contributions or expenditures, it would not qualify as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a). However, it may qualify as an independent expenditure committee or major donor committee under Section 82013(b) or (c).

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Two of your law firm's clients are the Tides Center, a tax-exempt entity under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(3), and Tides Advocacy, a tax-exempt entity under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(4). Both organizations act as fiscal sponsors for many projects engaged in substantive work that is consistent with the organizations' respective tax status. Under the fiscal sponsorship arrangement, the Tides Center and Tides Advocacy provide back-office, administrative, and compliance services, while the projects develop, direct, and manage their individual engagement strategies, missions, and day-to-day work. The projects raise their own funds and decide how those funds are spent, including whether those funds are spent on lobbying or campaign activities. As such, The Tides Center and Tides Advocacy do not devote any of their money to the projects by being their fiscal sponsor. The role of the Tides Center and Tides Advocacy related to these types of project activities is to ensure compliance with applicable law, including each respective organization's tax status and the disclosure provisions of both Federal and state law. You provided additional facts via email on June 19, 2019 stating that the projects do not have a formal, separate status with the Secretary of State. The projects are not required by the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy to adopt bylaws or articles relating to governance, yet some do. The Tides Center and Tides Advocacy require each project to have an advisory board with a minimum of three individuals. The advisory boards do not have a legal or fiduciary responsibility under the law, but they otherwise act as a fiduciary board would by setting executive director compensation and evaluating executive director performance.

File No. A-19-051
Page No. 3

In 2004, the Tides Center, received written advice from the Commission concerning its
obligations under the lobbying provisions of the Act. (Levikov Advice Letter, No. A-04-086.) At
the time, the Tides Center inquired whether it was required to report as a lobbyist employer. We
advised that it was not required to report as a lobbyist employer so long as it solely provided
management and administrative services to the projects and did not itself employ a lobbyist or
contract with a lobbying firm to lobby on its behalf. The facts and circumstances stated in that letter
have not changed.

In 2006, your law firm requested formal advice on behalf of the Tides Center under the
campaign provisions of the Act. (Fishburn Advice Letter, No. A-06-075.) At the time, one of the
projects of the Tides Center, the Children's Partnership ("TCP"), endorsed the qualification and
passage of a statewide initiative measure, the Tobacco Tax Act of 2006. Based on the applicable
law and regulations in effect at the time, we advised that TCP would have to disclose its in-kind
contributions and that TCP would qualify as a recipient committee, primarily formed to support the
qualification and/or passage of a statewide ballot measure under the now repealed "first bite of the
apple" rule. However, this advice was issued prior to the adoption of Section 84222, which has been
in effect since July 1, 2014.

Tides Advocacy, which operates in the same way as the Tides Center with respect to the
projects and its campaign-related activity, out of an abundance of caution while waiting for
guidance from the Commission, registered as a multipurpose organization general purpose
committee in 2018 to disclose contributions made by some of the projects. Tides Advocacy spends
available non-donor funds when engaging in campaign activity. Tides Advocacy spent the
organization's non-donor funds on one contribution in 2018. Decisions about the use of the
organizational funds, contrasted with project funds, are solely within the discretion of Tides
Advocacy and do not involve the projects or their funds.

ANALYSIS

Is the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy required to report as a Lobbyist Employer?

The Act regulates the activities of lobbyist employers. Specifically, Chapter 6 requires
lobbyist employers to register and file periodic reports disclosing money spent to influence
legislative or administrative action. (Sections 86100-86118.)

A lobbyist employer is defined in section 82039.5 as:

...any person,³ other than a lobbying firm, who:
(a) Employs one or more lobbyists for economic consideration, other than
reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, for the purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action, or

(b) Contracts for the services of a lobbying firm for economic consideration,

³ Section 82047 defines "person" as, among other things, a corporation. Both The Tides Center and Tides
Advocacy registered with the California Secretary of State's office as corporations.

File No. A-19-051
Page No. 4

other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expense[s], for the purpose of
influencing legislative or administrative action.

Under the facts presented, the individual projects control all programmatic activities for
their lobbying efforts, rather than the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy. However, the fact that the
Tides Center and Tides Advocacy are making the payments as part of their administrative functions
could qualify them as lobbyist employers under the Act's definition. Notably, Section 82037
defines "person" for purposes of the Act as "an individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint
venture, syndicate, business trust, company, corporation, limited liability company, association,
committee, and any other organization or group of persons acting in concert." Section 82039.5 does
not further define "person" for purposes of the lobbyist reporting provisions. To the extent that the
Tides Center or Tides Advocacy have no control over the lobbying efforts of an individual project,
we find it permissible that the project file as a lobbyist employer in lieu of the fiscal sponsor.

If a project makes an in-kind contribution must the fiscal sponsor report the contribution or does
the project disclose the contribution?

Multipurpose organizations, including any organization described in Section 501(c)(3) to
501(c)(10) of the Internal Revenue Code, must report pursuant to Section 84222 of the Act. Under
Section 84222, a multipurpose organization must report as a recipient committee under Section
82013(a) under any of the following circumstances:

(1) The multipurpose organization is a political committee registered with the
Federal Election Commission, except as provided in subdivision (a) of this
section, or a political committee registered with another state, and the
multipurpose organization makes contributions or expenditures in this state in
an amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of
Section 82013.

(2) The multipurpose organization solicits and receives payments from donors
in an amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of
Section 82013 for the purpose of making contributions or expenditures.

(3) The multipurpose organization accepts payments from donors in an amount
equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of Section
82013 subject to a condition, agreement, or understanding with the donor that
all or a portion of the payments may be used for making contributions or
expenditures.

(4) The multipurpose organization has existing funds from a donor and a
subsequent agreement or understanding is reached with the donor that all or a
portion of the funds may be used for making contributions or expenditures in an
amount equal to or greater than the amount identified in subdivision (a) of
Section 82013. The date of the subsequent agreement or understanding is
deemed to be the date of receipt of the payment.

File No. A-19-051
Page No. 5

(5) The multipurpose organization makes contributions or expenditures totaling
more than fifty thousand dollars ($50,000) in a period of 12 months or more
than one hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) in a period of four consecutive
calendar years."

A multipurpose organization that does not qualify as a recipient committee, may however
qualify as an independent expenditure committee or major donor committee if the organization
meets the thresholds provided in Section 82013(b) or (c). (See Section 84222(b).)

In adopting Section 84222, the Legislature further defined the reporting requirements for
multipurpose organizations including nonprofit entities. Moreover, in defining "multipurpose
organization," Section 84222 indicates that the obligation to report campaign activity rests with the
controlling nonprofit entity. The Tides Center and Tides Advocacy's individual projects do not
have separate nonprofit status. Accordingly, we do not find that Section 84222 permits an
individual project operating under the nonprofit status of a fiscal sponsor to report campaign
activity separately from the umbrella nonprofit entity.

Accordingly, as a multipurpose organization under Section 84222, the fiscal sponsor, either
the Tides Center or Tides Advocacy must report all campaign activity by any of its individual
projects. To the extent that a project makes an in-kind contribution, the fiscal sponsor qualifying as
a committee must report the campaign activity.

Does a project become a recipient committee if it continues to make in-kind contributions?

Multipurpose organizations, as defined in Section 84222(a), that receive donations or other
payments for purposes other than making political expenditures in California may qualify as
recipient committees once the organizations begin making political contributions and meet any of
the criteria specified in Section 84222(c).

As analyzed above, if a project meets any of the criteria specified in Section 84222(c), its
fiscal sponsor must register as a Section 82013(a) recipient committee.

If Tides Advocacy spends its nondonor funds on contributions going forward, should Tides
Advocacy remain registered as a general-purpose recipient committee or may it report as a major
donor?

Under Section 84222(c)(5)(A), a multipurpose organization shall not qualify as a recipient
committee if the organization makes contributions or expenditures using only available nondonor
funds. Accordingly, if Tides Advocacy makes contributions or expenditures using only available
non-donor funds, it is not required to register as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a).
However, it will need to file as an independent expenditure committee if it makes independent

File No. A-19-051
Page No. 6

expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year or a major donor committee if it makes
contributions totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar year. (Section 82013(b) and (c)).⁴

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Ryan O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:aja

⁴ A multipurpose organization that makes contributions or expenditures with non-donor funds is required to
briefly describe the source of the funds used on its major donor or independent expenditure report. (Section
84222(c)(5)(A).)
